articles.title: Heatwave warning for Gurgaon despite marginal dip in temperatures - The Times of India
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Haryana
articles.full_text:
Gurgaon: Despite a marginal dip in temperatures on Saturday, IMD warned of another spell of intense heat, with heatwave to severe heatwave conditions likely at several places in
Haryana, including Gurgaon, on Tuesday and Wednesday.
Parts of the city witnessed a brief spell of dust-storm and thunderstorms accompanied by light rainfall around 8.30am on Saturday. However, no weather observatory in Gurgaon recorded measurable rainfall and most of the day remained sunny and dry.
The maximum temperature in the city was recorded at 40.4°C, down from 41.5°C a day earlier. At Gurgaon AWS, the maximum settled at 40.1°C, down from 41.4°C on Friday, while NorthCap University recorded 41.5°C, down from 42.7°C. Night temperatures also saw a slight dip. The minimum temperature in Gurgaon was recorded at 27°C, down from 29.6°C the previous day. Gurgaon AWS recorded a minimum of 25°C against 26.3°C earlier, while NorthCap University logged 28.6°C, marginally higher than 28.5°C on Friday.
"The weather is likely to remain dry in Haryana from May 24 to 27. While there may not be any major change in temperatures over the next 24 hours, maximum temperatures are expected to rise by 2-4°C over the following four days before falling thereafter," IMD said.
For Sunday, the forecast shows that skies would remain partly cloudy, with minimum and maximum temperatures expected to hover around 29°C and 41°C, respectively.
Across Haryana, the average maximum temperature registered a fall of 1.2°C and remained near-normal levels.
Rohtak recorded the state's highest maximum temperature at 44.1°C.
According to IMD, weather conditions over northwest India are being influenced by multiple systems, including a western disturbance as a cyclonic circulation over Jammu and neighbouring areas in lower tropospheric levels. A trough extending from Punjab to southwest Rajasthan and an upper air cyclonic circulation over south Bihar and adjoining regions are also impacting regional weather patterns.
